---
title: "RoomRadar: Hotel Cancellation & Room Price Prediction"
subtitle: "Full Phase-2 Analysis — EDA, Classification, Regression, Calibration & Production API"
author: "Klimentina Chapovska"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    theme: cosmo
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

ROOT = Path("..").resolve()
sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT / "backend"))
FIG  = ROOT / "outputs" / "figures"
TBL  = ROOT / "outputs" / "tables"

df = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "Hotel Reservations.csv")
df["canceled"] = (df["booking_status"] == "Canceled").astype(int)
```

# Introduction

**RoomRadar** predicts two outcomes for hotel bookings made at an Indian hotel group:

1. **Cancellation probability** — will this booking be cancelled before arrival?
2. **Predicted room price** — what is the expected average room rate for this booking?

These predictions will be served through a FastAPI backend and displayed on a React dashboard deployed to Vercel.

This report documents the full data audit, exploratory analysis, and cleaning decisions that inform the modelling phase. No model has been trained yet.

---

# Dataset Overview

## Source & Provenance

The dataset is the [Kaggle Hotel Reservations Classification Dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/ahsan81/hotel-reservations-classification-dataset), stored at `data/raw/Hotel Reservations.csv` and never modified.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-schema
#| tbl-cap: "Column schema — 19 columns, 0 missing values"
schema = pd.read_csv(TBL / "01_column_summary.csv", index_col=0)
schema.index.name = "column"
schema.style \
    .format({"pct_null": "{:.2f}%"}) \
    .set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})
```

## Size & Completeness

```{python}
rows, cols = df.shape
n_missing  = df.isnull().sum().sum()
n_dupes    = df.duplicated().sum()

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": ["Rows", "Columns", "Missing values", "Duplicate rows"],
    "Value":  [f"{rows:,}", cols, n_missing, n_dupes],
})
summary.style.hide(axis="index")
```

The dataset is **complete**: no missing values and no duplicate rows across all 36,275 bookings.

## Target Variable

```{python}
#| label: fig-target
#| fig-cap: "Booking status distribution — moderate class imbalance"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "01_target_distribution.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-target
#| tbl-cap: "Booking status counts and proportions"
tbl = pd.read_csv(TBL / "02_target_distribution.csv", index_col=0)
tbl.columns = ["Count", "Pct (%)"]
tbl.style.format({"Count": "{:,}", "Pct (%)": "{:.2f}%"})
```

The cancellation rate is **32.8%** — a 2.1:1 ratio of not-cancelled to cancelled. This is a moderate class imbalance that requires stratified train/test splits. Class-weighted loss or oversampling will be evaluated after the baseline model is established.

---

# Data Quality Audit

## Schema Validation

All 19 expected columns are present and contain only expected categorical values. The `str` dtype reported by pandas 2.x for string columns is equivalent to the legacy `object` dtype — no actual mismatch.

## Suspicious Value Audit

```{python}
#| label: tbl-suspicious
#| tbl-cap: "Suspicious values flagged during audit"
audit = pd.read_csv(TBL / "03_suspicious_value_audit.csv", index_col=0)
audit = audit[["column", "issue", "n_rows", "pct"]].copy()
audit.columns = ["Column", "Issue", "# Rows", "% of Dataset"]
audit.style \
    .format({"# Rows": "{:,}", "% of Dataset": "{:.3f}%"}) \
    .hide(axis="index")
```

### Key findings

| Issue | Count | Severity | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero adults | 139 (0.38%) | Medium | Flag; keep |
| Zero price | 545 (1.50%) | High for price model | Flag; exclude from price model |
| Price IQR outliers (> €179.6) | 1,069 (2.95%) | Low | Keep; use log1p |
| Zero total nights | 78 (0.22%) | Low | Flag; keep |
| Lead time > 365 days | 243 (0.67%) | Low | Keep; use log1p |
| Calendar-invalid dates | 37 (0.10%) | High | Drop before training |
| Children > 5 | 3 (0.01%) | Negligible | Keep |

## Cleaning Decisions

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cleaning
#| tbl-cap: "Documented cleaning decisions with business reasoning"
clean = pd.read_csv(TBL / "16_cleaning_decisions.csv", index_col=0)
clean.style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})
```

---

# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Numeric Distributions

### Room Price

```{python}
#| label: fig-price
#| fig-cap: "Room price — right-skewed raw distribution; log1p normalises it"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "02_price_distribution.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 3.8), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The raw price distribution has a long right tail (skewness = 0.67) and 545 zero-price rows. Applying `log1p` produces a near-normal distribution suitable for linear models and stabilises gradient-based optimisers. Zero-price rows will be flagged separately so the model can learn their distinct pattern.

The Mann-Whitney U test confirms that cancelled and non-cancelled bookings have **significantly different price distributions** (p < 10⁻²⁰⁰) — room price is a meaningful predictor.

### Lead Time

```{python}
#| label: fig-lead
#| fig-cap: "Lead time — heavily right-skewed; log1p brings it close to normal"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "03_lead_time_distribution.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 3.8), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

Lead time (days between booking and arrival) is the **strongest single predictor** of cancellation (Spearman |r| = 0.42). Raw skewness is 1.29 — a log1p transform is applied for linear models; tree-based models are less sensitive but benefit from the compression of the extreme tail.

```{python}
#| label: fig-guests
#| fig-cap: "Guest and stay composition"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "04_guests_and_stay.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-numeric
#| tbl-cap: "Numeric feature summary statistics"
num = pd.read_csv(TBL / "04_numeric_summary.csv", index_col=0)
num[["mean","std","min","50%","max","skewness"]].round(2) \
    .style.format("{:.2f}").background_gradient(
        subset=["skewness"], cmap="RdYlGn_r", vmin=-1, vmax=5
    )
```

Notably high skewness:

- `no_of_children` — 4.71 (almost all bookings have 0 children)
- `no_of_previous_cancellations` — 25.2 (vast majority are 0)
- `no_of_previous_bookings_not_canceled` — 19.25 (same pattern)

These will be log-transformed or binarised in the feature engineering step.

## Categorical Profiles

```{python}
#| label: fig-cats
#| fig-cap: "Market segment and room type booking volumes"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "06_market_and_room_type.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-meal
#| fig-cap: "Meal plan selection"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "07_meal_plan.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

**Sparsity warnings:**

- `Room_Type 3` — only **7 rows**. One-hot encoding would create a near-zero-variance column. Target encoding on the training fold is the safer choice.
- `Meal Plan 3` — only **5 rows**. Same treatment.
- `Aviation` market segment — 125 rows (0.34%). Acceptable with target encoding.

---

## Cancellation Analysis

### By Market Segment

```{python}
#| label: fig-cancel-cat
#| fig-cap: "Cancellation rate by market segment and room type"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "08_cancel_rate_by_category.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cancel-mkt
#| tbl-cap: "Cancellation rate by market segment"
mkt = pd.read_csv(TBL / "06_cancel_rate_by_market_segment.csv", index_col=0)
mkt.columns = ["Market Segment", "Cancel Rate (%)", "N"]
mkt.style.format({"Cancel Rate (%)": "{:.1f}%", "N": "{:,}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

**Online** bookings cancel at 36.5% — nearly 4× the rate of **Corporate** bookings (10.9%). **Complementary** bookings have a 0% cancellation rate, which is expected (no financial cost to the guest for a comped stay, but they are presumably pre-screened).

### By Special Requests

```{python}
#| label: fig-cancel-sr
#| fig-cap: "Cancellation rate drops dramatically as special requests increase"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "09_cancel_rate_by_special_requests.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

Guests with **zero special requests** cancel at 43.2%; guests with 3 or more requests cancel at **0%**. This is one of the clearest signals in the dataset — guests who invest effort in customising their stay are far more committed. `no_of_special_requests` is the **second-strongest predictor** (|r| = 0.26).

### By Lead Time

```{python}
#| label: fig-cancel-lt
#| fig-cap: "Cancellation risk rises with lead time, especially beyond 60 days"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "10_lead_time_vs_cancellation.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cancel-lt
#| tbl-cap: "Cancellation rate by lead-time bucket"
lt = pd.read_csv(TBL / "10_cancel_rate_by_lead_time_bucket.csv", index_col=0)
lt.columns = ["Lead Time Bucket", "Cancel Rate (%)"]
lt.style.format({"Cancel Rate (%)": "{:.1f}%"}).hide(axis="index")
```

### Price vs Cancellation

```{python}
#| label: fig-price-cancel
#| fig-cap: "Cancelled bookings skew toward higher room prices"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "11_price_vs_cancellation.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

Cancelled bookings have **higher median prices** than non-cancelled ones. This may reflect guests who book at premium rates then find a better deal elsewhere — a common pattern in revenue management.

---

## Seasonality

```{python}
#| label: fig-season
#| fig-cap: "Monthly booking volume and cancellation rate"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "12_seasonality.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-price-month
#| fig-cap: "Average room price peaks in summer months (Jun–Sep)"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "13_price_by_month.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 3.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

Peak booking volume occurs in **August–October**. Cancellation rates are relatively stable across months (roughly 28–36%), which suggests lead time and booking channel are stronger drivers than seasonality per se. Room prices peak in summer (Jun–Sep), consistent with leisure demand.

---

## Correlation Structure

```{python}
#| label: fig-corr
#| fig-cap: "Spearman correlation matrix — lead time and special requests dominate"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "14_correlation_heatmap.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 8), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-corr-target
#| tbl-cap: "Top features correlated with cancellation (Spearman |r|)"
corr = pd.read_csv(TBL / "13_target_correlations.csv", index_col=0)
corr.columns = ["Feature", "|r| with Canceled"]
corr.style.format({"|r| with Canceled": "{:.3f}"}).hide(axis="index") \
    .background_gradient(subset=["|r| with Canceled"], cmap="Reds")
```

The two strongest predictors by Spearman correlation:

1. **`lead_time`** (|r| = 0.42) — the longer ahead a booking is made, the more likely it cancels
2. **`no_of_special_requests`** (|r| = 0.26) — more requests = greater commitment = lower cancellation

---

## Guest History

```{python}
#| label: fig-guest-hist
#| fig-cap: "Repeat guests cancel almost never; prior cancellations predict future ones"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "15_guest_history.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-repeat
#| tbl-cap: "Cancellation rate: new vs repeat guests"
rg = pd.read_csv(TBL / "14_cancel_rate_by_repeated_guest.csv", index_col=0)
rg = rg[["label", "cancel_rate_pct"]]
rg.columns = ["Guest Type", "Cancel Rate (%)"]
rg.style.format({"Cancel Rate (%)": "{:.1f}%"}).hide(axis="index")
```

**Repeat guests** cancel at just **1.7%** vs **33.6%** for new guests. This is a 20× difference and makes `repeated_guest` one of the most actionable signals. Hotels could offer loyalty incentives to convert new guests into repeat guests as a direct cancellation-reduction strategy.

---

# Feature Engineering Plan

The following features are **defined here but not computed yet** — they will be applied inside the training pipeline to prevent data leakage from validation and test folds.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-features
#| tbl-cap: "Planned engineered features with rationale"
feats = pd.read_csv(TBL / "15_feature_definitions.csv", index_col=0)
feats.columns = ["Feature", "Derivation", "Rationale"]
feats.style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{"text-align": "left", "font-size": "0.85em"})
```

---

# Business Insights

## Key Findings

1. **Lead time is the dominant cancellation signal.** Bookings made 3+ months ahead cancel at rates exceeding 50% in some segments. Hotels should consider non-refundable rate offers or deposit requirements for long-lead bookings.

2. **Special requests are a strong commitment signal.** A guest with 3+ requests has never cancelled in this dataset. Operationally, encouraging guests to add preferences (pillow type, floor preference, dietary needs) could reduce cancellations.

3. **Online channel drives 64% of bookings but 36.5% cancel.** Corporate bookings cancel at only 10.9%. There may be value in incentivising corporate contracts to reduce cancellation-driven revenue loss.

4. **Repeat guests almost never cancel (1.7%).** Loyalty programme investment has a direct ROI case here.

5. **Cancelled bookings have higher average prices.** Guests booking premium rooms are more likely to cancel — possibly because they find better deals or because the financial commitment causes second thoughts. Targeted pre-arrival communication for high-value bookings may help retention.

6. **Zero-price rows (n=545) need separate treatment.** These appear to be complimentary or staff bookings. They have distinct behaviour and should not inform the price regression model.

7. **37 calendar-invalid dates** (e.g. 30 Feb) must be dropped before training — they cannot represent valid arrivals.

## Recommended Cleaning Rules (Priority Order)

| Priority | Rule | Rows Affected | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Drop | Calendar-invalid arrival dates | 37 | Required |
| 2 — Drop | Zero-price rows from price model | 545 | Required for regression |
| 3 — Flag | Zero adults (`price_is_zero` analog) | 139 | Feature flag |
| 4 — Flag | Zero total nights | 78 | Feature flag |
| 5 — Transform | `log1p(lead_time)` | All | Reduces skew (1.29) |
| 6 — Transform | `log1p(avg_price_per_room)` | All | Reduces skew (0.67) |
| 7 — Encode | Rare categories via target encoding | ~12 rows | Prevents zero-variance cols |
| 8 — Stratify | Train/val/test splits | All | Preserves 32.8% cancel rate |

---

# Classification Pipeline

## Setup

```{python}
board   = pd.read_csv(TBL / "cl_07_leaderboard.csv", index_col=0)
splits  = pd.read_csv(TBL / "cl_01_split_sizes.csv", index_col=0)
fi      = pd.read_csv(TBL / "cl_06_feature_importance.csv", index_col=0)
sel     = pd.read_csv(TBL / "cl_05_selected_features.csv", index_col=0)
best_p  = pd.read_csv(TBL / "cl_04_xgb_best_params.csv", index_col=0)
ov      = pd.read_csv(TBL / "cl_02_dt_overfitting.csv", index_col=0)
```

## Data Splits

```{python}
#| label: tbl-splits
#| tbl-cap: "Stratified 60/20/20 split — cancellation rate preserved exactly"
splits.style.format({"n_rows": "{:,}", "n_canceled": "{:,}",
                     "pct_canceled": "{:.1f}%"}).hide(axis="index")
```

Each split preserves the 32.8 % cancellation rate exactly via stratification (`random_state=42`).

## Preprocessing Pipeline

Features pass through two sklearn stages inside each model pipeline:

1. **`FeatureEngineer`** (custom `TransformerMixin`) — adds 11 derived features (`total_nights`, `log_lead_time`, `log_price`, `price_is_zero`, `arrival_season`, `arrival_dow`, `prior_cancel_rate`, etc.) while preserving the originals for tree models.
2. **`ColumnTransformer`** — `StandardScaler` on 23 numeric columns; `OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='infrequent_if_exist', min_frequency=10)` on 3 categorical columns → 39 total features into each model.

**Leakage-safe feature selection** is applied inside the Logistic Regression pipeline as `SelectFromModel(XGBClassifier(n_estimators=50), threshold='mean')`. The selector sees only training-fold data during each CV split.

---

## Decision Tree — Overfitting Analysis

```{python}
#| label: fig-dt-overfit
#| fig-cap: "DT accuracy and F1 on train vs validation across max_depth"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "cl_01_dt_overfitting.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-dt-overfit
#| tbl-cap: "Decision Tree train vs validation metrics by depth (selected depths)"
show_depths = [1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 14, 19, 20]
ov_show = ov[ov["max_depth"].isin(show_depths)].copy()
ov_show.style \
    .format({c: "{:.4f}" for c in ov_show.columns if c != "max_depth"}) \
    .hide(axis="index") \
    .background_gradient(subset=["val_f1"], cmap="Greens")
```

The Decision Tree shows **classic overfitting**:

- Train accuracy climbs monotonically toward 100% as depth increases.
- Validation F1 peaks at **depth 19** (0.8091) then plateaus — the tree has learnt most of the recoverable signal by this point.
- The train–val **F1 gap** at depth 19 is `0.945 − 0.809 = 0.136`, confirming the model memorises training patterns that do not generalise fully.
- XGBoost's regularisation (`gamma=0.3`, `reg_lambda=3.0`) closes this gap to `0.931 − 0.830 = 0.101` while achieving higher absolute performance.

---

## XGBoost — Hyperparameter Tuning

```{python}
#| label: fig-xgb-cv
#| fig-cap: "RandomizedSearchCV: top-10 configurations by 5-fold CV ROC-AUC"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "cl_02_xgb_cv_results.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-xgb-params
#| tbl-cap: "Best XGBoost hyperparameters (30 iterations, 5-fold stratified CV)"
best_p.columns = ["Parameter", "Value"]
best_p.style.hide(axis="index")
```

Search space covered n_estimators, max_depth, learning_rate, subsample, colsample_bytree, min_child_weight, gamma, reg_alpha, reg_lambda across 30 random configurations with 5-fold stratified CV scored on ROC-AUC. Best CV ROC-AUC = **0.9514**.

---

## Feature Selection (Logistic Regression)

```{python}
#| label: tbl-sel-features
#| tbl-cap: "Features selected by SelectFromModel — 14 of 39 kept"
sel_yes = sel[sel["selected"] == True]["feature"].reset_index(drop=True)
sel_no  = sel[sel["selected"] == False]["feature"].reset_index(drop=True)
n_pad   = max(len(sel_yes), len(sel_no))
pd.DataFrame({
    "Selected (14)": list(sel_yes) + [""] * (n_pad - len(sel_yes)),
    "Dropped (25)":  list(sel_no)  + [""] * (n_pad - len(sel_no)),
}).style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{"font-size": "0.85em"})
```

The leakage-safe selector (XGB with 50 trees, threshold=mean importance) retains 14 of 39 post-OHE features. Notably selected: `lead_time`, `avg_price_per_room`, `no_of_special_requests`, `arrival_season`, `market_segment_type_Online/Offline` — all confirmed strong predictors in EDA.

---

## Model Leaderboard

```{python}
#| label: fig-leaderboard
#| fig-cap: "All five test-set metrics across four models"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "cl_06_leaderboard.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 4.5), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-leaderboard
#| tbl-cap: "Full leaderboard — test set (positive class = Canceled)"
show_cols = ["model", "train_f1", "val_f1", "test_accuracy",
             "test_precision", "test_recall", "test_f1", "test_roc_auc"]
board[show_cols].style \
    .format({c: "{:.4f}" for c in show_cols if c != "model"}) \
    .hide(axis="index") \
    .background_gradient(subset=["test_roc_auc"], cmap="Greens") \
    .background_gradient(subset=["test_f1"],      cmap="Blues")
```

| Model | Test Acc | Test F1 | Test AUC | Train–Val F1 gap |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **XGBoost** | **0.882** | **0.827** | **0.953** | 0.10 |
| Decision Tree | 0.861 | 0.795 | 0.876 | 0.14 |
| Logistic Regression | 0.777 | 0.696 | 0.863 | 0.00 |
| Dummy | 0.672 | 0.000 | 0.500 | — |

**XGBoost wins on every metric.** Its AUC of 0.953 means it correctly ranks a random cancelled booking above a random non-cancelled booking 95.3% of the time.

---

## Confusion Matrices — Test Set

```{python}
#| label: fig-cms
#| fig-cap: "Confusion matrices — test set (2,376 Canceled, 4,872 Not_Canceled)"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "cl_03_confusion_matrices.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15, 4), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

XGBoost misclassifies **281 cancellations as not-cancelled** (false negatives) and **578 non-cancellations as cancelled** (false positives) out of 7,248 test rows. The class-weight and `scale_pos_weight=2` settings keep recall competitive at ~83%.

---

## ROC Curves — Test Set

```{python}
#| label: fig-roc
#| fig-cap: "ROC curves — XGBoost dominates across all operating points"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "cl_04_roc_curves.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 7), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

XGBoost's ROC curve lies above all other models at every threshold. The gap between XGBoost (0.953) and Decision Tree (0.876) is substantial — regularised ensembles dominate on this dataset.

---

## XGBoost Feature Importances

```{python}
#| label: fig-fi
#| fig-cap: "XGBoost gain importances — top 20 features"
img = mpimg.imread(FIG / "cl_05_feature_importance.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7), dpi=120)
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-fi
#| tbl-cap: "Top 10 XGBoost feature importances"
fi.head(10).style.format({"importance": "{:.4f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

**`market_segment_type_Online`** is the single most important feature (gain = 0.233), consistent with online bookings having a 36.5% cancellation rate vs 10.9% for corporate. **`required_car_parking_space`** ranks second — guests who request parking are significantly less likely to cancel (a proxy for commitment). **`no_of_special_requests`** and its binary version `has_special_request` both appear in the top 10, confirming the EDA finding.

---

# Business Insights

## Key Findings

1. **Lead time is the dominant cancellation signal.** Bookings made 3+ months ahead cancel at rates exceeding 50% in some segments. Hotels should consider non-refundable rate offers or deposit requirements for long-lead bookings.

2. **Special requests are a strong commitment signal.** A guest with 3+ requests has never cancelled in this dataset. Operationally, encouraging guests to add preferences (pillow type, floor preference, dietary needs) could reduce cancellations.

3. **Online channel drives 64% of bookings but 36.5% cancel.** Corporate bookings cancel at only 10.9%. There may be value in incentivising corporate contracts to reduce cancellation-driven revenue loss.

4. **Repeat guests almost never cancel (1.7%).** Loyalty programme investment has a direct ROI case here.

5. **Cancelled bookings have higher average prices.** Guests booking premium rooms are more likely to cancel — possibly because they find better deals or because the financial commitment causes second thoughts. Targeted pre-arrival communication for high-value bookings may help retention.

6. **Zero-price rows (n=545) need separate treatment.** These appear to be complimentary or staff bookings. They have distinct behaviour and should not inform the price regression model.

7. **37 calendar-invalid dates** (e.g. 30 Feb) must be dropped before training — they cannot represent valid arrivals.

## Recommended Cleaning Rules (Priority Order)

| Priority | Rule | Rows Affected | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Drop | Calendar-invalid arrival dates | 37 | Required |
| 2 — Drop | Zero-price rows from price model | 545 | Required for regression |
| 3 — Flag | Zero adults (`price_is_zero` analog) | 139 | Feature flag |
| 4 — Flag | Zero total nights | 78 | Feature flag |
| 5 — Transform | `log1p(lead_time)` | All | Reduces skew (1.29) |
| 6 — Transform | `log1p(avg_price_per_room)` | All | Reduces skew (0.67) |
| 7 — Encode | Rare categories via OHE infrequent | ~12 rows | Prevents zero-variance cols |
| 8 — Stratify | Train/val/test splits | All | Preserves 32.8% cancel rate |

---

# Cancellation Model Finalization

This section covers probability calibration, decision threshold selection, risk band
definition, and the final production model artefacts.

Script: `ml/scripts/finalize_classification.py`

```{python}
PROD_TBL = ROOT / "outputs" / "tables"
PROD_FIG = ROOT / "outputs" / "figures"
import json
META = json.loads((ROOT / "models" / "cancellation_metadata.json").read_text())
```

## Probability Calibration

A well-trained classifier can produce mis-calibrated probabilities — the raw
XGBoost score at 0.7 does not necessarily mean a 70 % cancellation rate.
We evaluated two post-hoc calibrators on the **validation set only**:

- **Isotonic regression** — monotone non-parametric recalibration
- **Sigmoid (Platt scaling)** — logistic regression on the raw scores

Both are fitted to the same 7,248-row validation set. The test set is never
opened during this step.

### Brier Scores

```{python}
cal_tbl = pd.read_csv(PROD_TBL / "prod_01_calibration_brier_scores.csv", index_col=0)
cal_tbl.style.format({"brier_score": "{:.5f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

*Lower Brier score = better probability accuracy.  Isotonic calibration wins.*

### Reliability Diagrams

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
img = mpimg.imread(PROD_FIG / "prod_01_reliability_curves.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*A perfect calibrator would follow the diagonal. Isotonic (right panel) is much
closer to ideal than uncalibrated (left panel), especially in the 40–80 % range
where the hotel's revenue risk is highest.*

## Decision Threshold Selection

The default 0.5 threshold is rarely optimal for imbalanced binary classification.
We searched thresholds from 0.05 to 0.85 on the validation set and chose the one
that **maximises F1** for the positive class (Canceled).

```{python}
thresh_tbl = pd.read_csv(PROD_TBL / "prod_02_threshold_search_val.csv", index_col=0)
best = thresh_tbl.loc[thresh_tbl["f1"].idxmax()]
print(f"Chosen threshold: {best['threshold']:.2f}   "
      f"Val F1={best['f1']:.4f}   Precision={best['precision']:.4f}   Recall={best['recall']:.4f}")
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
img = mpimg.imread(PROD_FIG / "prod_02_threshold_analysis.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*The F1 curve peaks at **threshold = 0.34** — lower than 0.5 because the
positive class (Canceled, 32.8 %) is the minority.  Lowering the threshold
recovers more true cancellations at a modest cost to precision.*

## Risk Band Definitions

We overlay three business risk bands on top of the calibrated probability:

| Band | Probability range | Business action |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | < 30 % | No action required |
| **Medium** | 30 – 60 % | Pre-arrival confirmation, offer flexible upgrade |
| **High** | > 60 % | Require deposit or issue retention incentive |

```{python}
band_tbl = pd.read_csv(PROD_TBL / "prod_03_risk_band_distribution.csv", index_col=0)
band_tbl[band_tbl["split"]=="test"].drop(columns="split").style.hide(axis="index")
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
img = mpimg.imread(PROD_FIG / "prod_03_risk_bands.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*On the test set, 63 % of bookings fall in the Low band with only a 6.6 % actual
cancellation rate — virtually no hotel action needed.  The High band captures 29 %
of bookings with an 87 % actual cancellation rate, giving operations teams a very
reliable alert signal.*

## Final Test-Set Evaluation

The test set was opened **once** after all calibration and threshold decisions were
locked on the validation set.

### Uncalibrated vs Calibrated on Test Set

```{python}
cmp_tbl = pd.read_csv(PROD_TBL / "prod_05_raw_vs_calibrated_test.csv", index_col=0)
cmp_tbl.style.format({
    "accuracy": "{:.4f}", "precision": "{:.4f}", "recall": "{:.4f}",
    "f1": "{:.4f}", "roc_auc": "{:.4f}", "brier": "{:.5f}"
}).hide(axis="index")
```

*Calibration improves the Brier score (probability quality) without meaningfully
changing classification metrics — the model's ranking ability (AUC) is unchanged.*

### Confusion Matrices

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
img = mpimg.imread(PROD_FIG / "prod_04_confusion_matrix_final.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### ROC Curve with Operating Point

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))
img = mpimg.imread(PROD_FIG / "prod_05_roc_final.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Overfitting: Honest Assessment

```{python}
ov_tbl = pd.read_csv(PROD_TBL / "prod_04_overfitting_summary.csv", index_col=0)
ov_tbl.style.format({
    "accuracy": "{:.4f}", "precision": "{:.4f}", "recall": "{:.4f}",
    "f1": "{:.4f}", "roc_auc": "{:.4f}", "brier": "{:.5f}"
})
```

**Summary:**

- **Train → Val F1 gap = 0.099** — XGBoost memorises some training patterns despite
  regularisation (`gamma=0.3`, `reg_lambda=3.0`).  This is expected for a 500-tree
  ensemble on 21 k rows.
- **Val → Test F1 gap = 0.005** — negligible.  Val and test metrics are nearly
  identical, confirming that the model generalises well and that threshold/calibration
  choices made on the validation set are not overfitted.
- **ROC-AUC is stable**: 0.9921 (train) → 0.9531 (val) → 0.9523 (test).  The
  ranking ability generalises well; the gap is mostly in the tails of the probability
  distribution where the model is overconfident on training data.
- **Calibration reduces the Brier score** from 0.0840 (uncal) to 0.0792 (cal)
  on the test set, confirming that the isotonic calibrator generalises beyond val.

## Saved Production Artefacts

```{python}
import os
model_size = os.path.getsize(ROOT / "models" / "cancellation_model.pkl") / 1024
print(f"cancellation_model.pkl          {model_size:.1f} KB")
print(f"cancellation_metadata.json      {os.path.getsize(ROOT / 'models' / 'cancellation_metadata.json') / 1024:.1f} KB")
print(f"\nThreshold : {META['threshold']}")
print(f"Calibration: {META['calibration_method']}")
print(f"Brier score: {META['brier_calibrated']} (calibrated)  "
      f"{META['brier_uncalibrated']} (raw)")
print(f"Test AUC   : {META['test_metrics']['roc_auc']}")
print(f"Test F1    : {META['test_metrics']['f1']}")
```

The pipeline can be loaded and used for inference with:

```python
import joblib, sys, types
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, "ml/scripts")
from calibration_pipeline import CalibratedPipeline
from transformers import FeatureEngineer, RegFeatureEngineer

# Register stub modules so joblib can deserialise pickles by original class paths
_s = types.ModuleType("train_classification"); _s.FeatureEngineer = FeatureEngineer
sys.modules.setdefault("train_classification", _s)

model = joblib.load("models/cancellation_model.pkl")
prob  = model.predict_proba(booking_df)[0, 1]  # calibrated cancellation probability
```

---

# Room Price Regression Pipeline

Predicts `avg_price_per_room` (€) for a hotel booking.

Script: `ml/scripts/train_regression.py`

**Cleaning applied (regression-specific):**

| Rule | Rows | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Drop invalid calendar dates | -37 | Same as classification |
| Drop zero-price rows | -545 | No meaningful price signal; distort regression |
| **Training set** | **21,415** | After both exclusions |

**Excluded from predictors:** `Booking_ID`, `booking_status`, `avg_price_per_room`,
`log_price`, `price_is_zero` — the last two would directly leak the target.

```{python}
RG_TBL = ROOT / "outputs" / "tables"
RG_FIG = ROOT / "outputs" / "figures"
```

## Data Splits

```{python}
split_tbl = pd.read_csv(RG_TBL / "rg_01_split_sizes.csv", index_col=0)
split_tbl.style.format({"mean": "{:.2f}", "median": "{:.2f}", "std": "{:.2f}",
                         "min": "{:.2f}", "max": "{:.2f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

All three splits have nearly identical mean (≈ €105) and std (≈ €33), confirming
the simple random 60/20/20 split is well-balanced for regression.

## Target Transformation: Raw vs log1p

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4.5))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIG / "rg_01_target_distribution.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
target_tbl = pd.read_csv(RG_TBL / "rg_02_target_comparison.csv", index_col=0)
target_tbl.style.format({"r2": "{:.4f}", "mae": "{:.2f}", "rmse": "{:.2f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

*The log1p transformation reduces right skew (skew drops from ≈ 1.2 to ≈ −0.1)
and marginally improves Ridge validation RMSE (€23.04 vs €23.05).  All models are
therefore trained on log1p(y); predictions are back-transformed with `expm1` before
all metric calculations and comparisons.*

## Decision Tree Overfitting

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIG / "rg_02_dt_overfitting.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
ov_dt = pd.read_csv(RG_TBL / "rg_03_dt_overfitting.csv", index_col=0)
ov_dt.style.format({"train_rmse": "{:.2f}", "val_rmse": "{:.2f}", "gap": "{:.2f}"})
```

*Validation RMSE bottoms at depth 12 (RMSE ≈ €20.41). Beyond that, additional
splits fit noise — train RMSE collapses to €7.61 at depth 20 while val RMSE
rises to €21.29, a gap of €13.68.  The tree at depth 12 is used in the
model comparison.*

## XGBoost Hyperparameter Tuning

```{python}
xgb_tbl = pd.read_csv(RG_TBL / "rg_04_xgb_best_params.csv", index_col=0)
xgb_tbl.style.hide(axis="index")
```

*30 random combinations tested with 5-fold KFold CV on the training set.
Best CV RMSE ≈ 0.188 (log-space); equivalent to ≈ €20 back-transformed.
Moderate depth (7) with light subsampling (0.6) and L2 regularisation
prevent overfitting.*

## Test-Set Leaderboard

```{python}
leader = pd.read_csv(RG_TBL / "rg_06_leaderboard_test.csv", index_col=0)
leader.style.format({
    "train_r2": "{:.4f}", "train_mae": "{:.2f}", "train_rmse": "{:.2f}",
    "test_r2": "{:.4f}", "test_mae":  "{:.2f}", "test_rmse":  "{:.2f}",
}).hide(axis="index").highlight_min(subset=["test_rmse"], color="#fce4e4")\
  .highlight_max(subset=["test_r2"],   color="#e8f8e8")
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 4.5))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIG / "rg_06_leaderboard_bar.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**XGBoost is the clear winner:** test RMSE €17.48, MAE €11.83, R² 0.716.
This means the model explains 71.6 % of price variance and is off by ~€12 on average.

| Model | Train RMSE | Test RMSE | Overfit gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| DummyRegressor | €33.21 | €33.11 | — |
| Ridge | €22.86 | €23.18 | €0.32 |
| DecisionTree (d=12) | €16.46 | €20.58 | €4.12 |
| **XGBoost (tuned)** | **€15.97** | **€17.48** | €1.51 |

## Predicted vs Actual

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 10))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIG / "rg_03_predicted_vs_actual.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*All models under-predict at the high end (>€200) — a common pattern when the
training set has few extreme observations.  XGBoost handles the mid-range (€50–€200)
well, with predictions tightly clustered around the diagonal.*

## Residual Analysis (XGBoost)

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15, 4.5))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIG / "rg_04_residuals_xgb.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*Residuals are centred near zero with some right-skew at high prices (the model
consistently under-predicts luxury rooms).  The QQ plot shows light tails — the
distribution is approximately normal in the core range but has heavier tails than
a Gaussian.*

## Feature Importances (XGBoost)

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIG / "rg_05_feature_importances.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
fi = pd.read_csv(RG_TBL / "rg_07_feature_importances_xgb.csv", index_col=0)
fi.head(10).style.format({"importance": "{:.4f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

**Top predictors of room price:**

1. **room_type_reserved (Room_Type 1)** — single largest driver; premium room types
   command 30–50 % price premiums
2. **total_guests** — more guests → larger rooms → higher price
3. **market_segment_type** — Online and Complementary segments have different effective
   pricing compared to Corporate
4. **arrival_season** — seasonal demand affects rack rates
5. **type_of_meal_plan** — meal inclusions add to the effective room price

These align with standard hotel revenue management theory.

## Overfitting Summary

| Model | Train R² | Test R² | Train RMSE | Test RMSE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DummyRegressor | −0.02 | −0.02 | €33.21 | €33.11 |
| Ridge | 0.52 | 0.50 | €22.86 | €23.18 |
| DecisionTree (d=12) | 0.75 | 0.61 | €16.46 | €20.58 |
| XGBoost (tuned) | 0.76 | 0.72 | €15.97 | €17.48 |

**XGBoost overfitting is minimal** (train R² 0.764 → test R² 0.716, gap = 0.048).
Regularisation (`gamma=0.1`, `reg_lambda=1.0`, `subsample=0.6`) is working.

**Decision Tree overfits more** despite using optimal depth 12 — the train/test
RMSE gap is €4.12 vs €1.51 for XGBoost.

**Ceiling analysis:** R² = 0.716 leaves 28.4 % unexplained variance.  The missing
signal is likely contextual: special promotions, loyalty pricing, group discounts,
and competitor rates — none of which are available in this dataset.

---

# Room Price Model Finalization

This section documents the pre-save validation steps and the final production
regression model artefacts.

Script: `ml/scripts/finalize_regression.py`

```{python}
import json as _json
REG_META = _json.loads((ROOT / "models" / "room_price_metadata.json").read_text())
RG_FIN_TBL = ROOT / "outputs" / "tables"
RG_FIN_FIG = ROOT / "outputs" / "figures"
```

## Pre-save Validation

### 1 — Parameter name verification

```{python}
print(f"reg_alpha verified: {REG_META['reg_alpha_verified']}")
print(f"XGBoost params stored: {list(REG_META['xgb_params'].keys())}")
```

`reg_alpha` and `reg_lambda` are confirmed correct for the XGBoost 3.3 sklearn API.
The `alpha`/`lambda` aliases exist only in the native booster; the sklearn
`XGBRegressor` uses the `reg_` prefix throughout.

### 2 — Currency note

The project data dictionary annotates `avg_price_per_room` with a `$` symbol
(e.g., "max $540") but the original Kaggle dataset does not specify a currency.
All monetary values are therefore labelled **price units** in model outputs and
metadata to avoid misrepresenting the denomination.

### 3 — Target transformation: tuned XGBoost on validation

The training pipeline (`train_regression.py`) compared raw vs log1p(y) using
Ridge regression only.  Here we compare them using the **tuned XGBoost
hyperparameters** on the validation set — no test data used.

```{python}
cmp = pd.read_csv(RG_FIN_TBL / "rg_fin_01_target_comparison_xgb_val.csv", index_col=0)
cmp.style.format({"r2": "{:.4f}", "mae": "{:.3f}", "rmse": "{:.3f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.5))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIN_FIG / "rg_fin_01_target_comparison_xgb.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Result:** The **raw target** outperforms log1p for the tuned XGBoost
(val RMSE 17.037 vs 18.045, Δ = 1.008 price units).  This reverses the earlier
Ridge-based finding — XGBoost's tree structure handles the slightly right-skewed
price distribution directly without needing the log transformation.  The
production model is therefore trained on the **raw target**.

### 4 — Corrected DummyRegressor baseline

The previous training run trained `DummyRegressor` on the log1p target, making
it predict the *geometric mean* of prices.  The correct baseline predicts the
*arithmetic mean* of the raw training prices.

```{python}
dummy_tbl = pd.read_csv(RG_FIN_TBL / "rg_fin_02_dummy_baseline_raw.csv", index_col=0)
print(f"Arithmetic mean of training prices: {REG_META['dummy_baseline']['train_mean']:.2f} price units")
dummy_tbl.style.format({"r2": "{:.4f}", "mae": "{:.3f}", "rmse": "{:.3f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

The corrected Dummy val RMSE (33.226) is close to the log1p version (33.570) —
the difference is small but the arithmetic mean is the correct conceptual baseline.

### 5 — Regenerated feature importances (production model)

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIN_FIG / "rg_fin_02_feature_importances_production.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
fi_prod = pd.read_csv(RG_FIN_TBL / "rg_fin_03_feature_importances_clean.csv", index_col=0)
fi_prod.head(10).style.format({"importance": "{:.5f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

**Key shift from log1p → raw target model:**

| Rank | Raw target model | log1p model |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | total_guests (0.128) | room_type_reserved_Room_Type_1 (0.136) |
| 2 | room_type_reserved_Room_Type_6 (0.111) | total_guests (0.127) |
| 3 | room_type_reserved_Room_Type_1 (0.107) | market_segment_type_Online (0.092) |
| 4 | room_type_reserved_Room_Type_7 (0.069) | market_segment_type_Complementary (0.068) |
| 5 | type_of_meal_plan_Meal_Plan_2 (0.065) | room_type_reserved_Room_Type_4 (0.065) |

Party size (`total_guests`) moves to first place and Room_Type_6 / _7 become more
prominent — these are the higher-priced suite categories that have larger absolute
price effects, which the raw model captures directly.

## Final Test-Set Evaluation

The test set was opened exactly once, after all target-transformation and
hyperparameter decisions were locked.

```{python}
final_tbl = pd.read_csv(RG_FIN_TBL / "rg_fin_04_final_metrics.csv", index_col=0)
final_tbl.style.format({"r2": "{:.4f}", "mae": "{:.3f}", "rmse": "{:.3f}"}).hide(axis="index")
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))
img = mpimg.imread(RG_FIN_FIG / "rg_fin_03_final_test_evaluation.png")
ax.imshow(img); ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

| Metric | Dummy (raw mean) | XGBoost (raw, tuned) |
|---|---|---|
| Test R² | −0.0002 | **0.755** |
| Test MAE | 25.16 price units | **10.46 price units** |
| Test RMSE | 32.81 price units | **16.24 price units** |

XGBoost reduces RMSE from the dummy baseline by **16.57 price units (51 %)**.

**Overfitting:**

| Split | R² | RMSE |
|---|---|---|
| Train | 0.894 | 10.72 |
| Val | 0.737 | 17.04 |
| Test | **0.755** | **16.24** |

Train→Val R² gap = 0.157 (moderate — XGBoost memorises some training noise).
Val→Test gap = −0.018 (test slightly *better* than val — within normal sampling
variation for splits of this size; the model generalises cleanly).

## Saved Production Artefacts

```{python}
import os as _os
price_size = _os.path.getsize(ROOT / "models" / "room_price_model.pkl") / 1024
meta_size  = _os.path.getsize(ROOT / "models" / "room_price_metadata.json") / 1024
print(f"room_price_model.pkl         {price_size:.1f} KB")
print(f"room_price_metadata.json     {meta_size:.1f} KB")
print(f"\nTarget transformation : {REG_META['target_transformation']}")
print(f"Predict inverse       : {REG_META['predict_inverse_transform']}")
print(f"Val  R² / RMSE        : {REG_META['val_metrics']['r2']} / {REG_META['val_metrics']['rmse']}")
print(f"Test R² / RMSE        : {REG_META['test_metrics']['r2']} / {REG_META['test_metrics']['rmse']}")
print(f"Dummy val RMSE        : {REG_META['dummy_baseline']['val_rmse']} (arithmetic mean baseline)")
```

The pipeline can be loaded and used for inference with:

```python
import joblib, sys, types
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, "ml/scripts")
from transformers import RegFeatureEngineer

_s = types.ModuleType("train_regression"); _s.RegFeatureEngineer = RegFeatureEngineer
sys.modules.setdefault("train_regression", _s)

model = joblib.load("models/room_price_model.pkl")
price = float(model.predict(booking_df)[0])  # price in original units (no back-transform needed)
```

**Known limitations:**

```{python}
for i, lim in enumerate(REG_META["limitations"], 1):
    print(f"{i}. {lim}")
```

---

# Project Status

All phases complete:

- [x] **EDA & data audit** — cleaning decisions documented, 37 invalid dates removed, zero-price rows flagged
- [x] **Classification pipeline** — 4 models, XGBoost tuned (AUC 0.953), probability calibrated, risk bands defined
- [x] **Regression pipeline** — 4 models, XGBoost tuned (R² 0.755, RMSE 16.24 price units)
- [x] **Production API** — FastAPI `/predict` endpoint, both models served in a single request
- [x] **React dashboard** — interactive EDA charts, model performance page, live prediction form
- [x] **Deployment** — frontend live at <https://roomradar-henna.vercel.app>, backend on Render
